text: Merleau-Ponty analyzed the experience of a blind man using a cane and found something philosophically striking: after practice, the man does not feel the cane; he feels through it to the texture of the pavement. The cane has been absorbed into the body schema and extended its reach. This assimilation demonstrates what Merleau-Ponty called 'motor intentionality' — the body's capacity to incorporate instruments into its pre-reflective directedness toward the world. The same principle applies to digital devices: smartphones begin as tools held at arm's length but, through habitual use, become prosthetic extensions of attention itself, redirecting intentionality before conscious choice intervenes.
question: What does the blind man's experience with a cane illustrate, according to Merleau-Ponty?

options.text: That tactile perception is more reliable than visual perception for navigation
That tools must remain at arm's length to preserve conscious intentionality
That practiced instruments are absorbed into the body schema, extending its reach
That motor intentionality depends on reflective awareness of the instrument's weight
